text:
Admission Date:  2007-12-18              Discharge Date:   1993-08-01


Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Aspirin / Percocet / Codeine / Nutren Pulmonary

Attending:Joshua
Chief Complaint:
Urosepsis and pneumonia

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
PICC line placement 2018-03-29


History of Present Illness:
85F from Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med with reports of R facial droop and slurred
speech. Began vs. noticed at 9:30a. At the NH, she was
hypotensive with a BP of 60/40, which improved to 80/60 with
500ml NS. In the ED, code stroke called but head CT showed no
bleed. Neurology was consulted and felt that she did not suffer
a large stroke and as such, no TPA was indicated. Was on a
course of levofloxacin at Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med for UTI. In the ED, she
received vanco, ceftaz, steroids, 1 unit PRBCs, and tylenol for
hypotension and presumed sepsis.
.
She was admitted to ICU for sepsis secondary to PNA and UTI.
Given her h/o multidrug resistant pseudomonas UTIs, she was
started on meropenem with clinical improvement.  Urine cultures
from day of admission, however, revealed pseudomonas was
resistant to meropenem.  Given her clinical improvement,
concentration of meropenem in the urine might be adequate to
treat so she has been continued on it.  The lab is currently
performing additional antibiotic sensitivities.  Additionally,
cultures from a recent bronchial washing revealed MRSA and,
given pneumonia on imaging and clinically with associated
sepsis, she has been continued on vancomycin since admission.
.
She is being transferred to the care of her primary cardiologist
for further management of the above issues while hospitalized.
.
ROS: denies fevers, chills, shortness of breath, dysuria,
frequency, hematuria, hematochezia, chest pain, admits to
shortness of breath, but says is baseline, admits to fatigue and
weakness. Complains of pain on right thigh secondary to zoster.


Past Medical History:
1)Asthma > 5 hospitalization with no history of intubations. She
has been on steroids since the beginning of 25. Prior to
this, she had been steroid free for the past 2 years. Recent
hospitalization with intubation complicated by MRSA pneumonia,
d/c on 07-26 to rehab.
2)Hypertension.
3)Steroid induced hyperglycemia. Discharged on insulin following

her Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med admission.
4)Peripheral vascular disease, status post left fem-peroneal
bypass in 1996
5)Multi-focal bacterial pneumonia.
6)Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease- PFT 01-23- FVC 61% pred,

FEV1 56% pred, FEV1/FVC 92%, Reduced FVC related to gas
trapping, ~400 cc worse than PFT from one year ago.
7)Multi-focal atrial tachycardia.
8)Oral thrush.
9)Question left hilar mass.
10)Mult aspirations in past requiring now being on feeding tube

11)Hx. MRSA PNA
12) Bell's Palsy
13) UTI
14) GIB


Social History:
Denies history of smoking. Only social alcohol, ~3 drinks /week.
No other drug use. Widowed, with 3 children and 8 grandchildren.



Family History:
Asthma in her father

Physical Exam:
T 97.1 HR 89 BP 131/66 O2 sat 99% on 2L NC Weight 61 kg
Gen: pleasant cooperative NAD
HEENT: very dry MM, PERRL
Cor: tachycardic, irregularly irregular, II/VI systolic murmur
heard loudest at the apex and radiating to L axilla
Pulm: tachypneic, diffuse rhonchi, egophany in the RLL field
Abd: +BS, soft/NT/ND, JPEG in place
Ext: 2+ doughy edema B/L LE, dressing in place over stasis
ulcers on B/L LE (L appears superficial, 1cm; R shows some
muscle, but appears to be healing well), strength lower
extremities 09-04 bilaterally, upper extremities 11-11 bilaterally.
Skin: fragile skin, chronic venous stasis changes, scattered
ecchymoses, xerotic
Neuro: thinks she was recently in [**State 108**]. Ptosis of right eye,
mild right facial droop. Sensation intact. No tongue deviation.

Pertinent Results:
2007-12-18 Portable CXR: Persistent left lower lobe opacity and
effusion. 2.Similar rounded right infrahilar density, previously
felt to represent rounded atelectasis. 3. New right lower lobe
opacity which may represent recurrence of atelectasis or
pneumonia.
.
2007-12-18 Head CT: 1.  No hemorrhage.  Chronic microvascular
ischemic changes. 2.  Sinus disease as noted above.
.
EKG: ectopic rhythm with right axis and right bundle branch
block
.
2019-02-03 CHEST CT: IMPRESSION:
1. Limited assessment for hilar mass due to absence of
intravenous contrast. If the patient has strict
contraindications to iodinated contrast, MRI of the chest should
be considered to answer the clinical question of hilar mass.
2. Confluent rounded opacity in right infrahilar region which
has developed since 2015-03-26 and probably represents a
combination of round atelectasis and reactive lymph nodes, but
is not fully characterized on this unenhanced CT scan.
3. Persistent lower lobe bronchial impaction and combination of
atelectasis and consolidation, in keeping with the provided
history of recurrent aspiration pneumonia. New multifocal
centrilobular nodular opacities likely reflect a diffuse
bronchiolitis, either from aspiration or infectious etiology.
4. New small amount of ascites, development of anasarca, and
slight increase in small bilateral pleural effusions.
5. Diffuse mesenteric stranding throughout the imaged portion of
the upper abdomen, a nonspecific finding. If the patient
undergoes a contrast enhanced CT of the chest or MRI, inclusion
of the abdomen may be considered to more completely evaluate
these structures if warranted clinically.
6. Diffuse coronary artery calcifications.
.
2015-03-26 Echocardiogram:
The left atrium is normal in size. The left ventricular cavity
size is normal.  Overall left ventricular systolic function is
normal (LVEF>55%). Right  ventricular chamber size and free wall
motion are normal. The aortic root is  mildly dilated. The
aortic valve leaflets are moderately thickened. There is  mild
aortic valve stenosis. No aortic regurgitation is seen. The
mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. Mild to moderate
(04-18+) mitral regurgitation is seen. [Due to acoustic shadowing,
the severity of mitral regurgitation may be significantly
UNDERestimated.] The left ventricular inflow pattern suggests
impaired relaxation. The tricuspid valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. The estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure is
normal. There is a very small pericardial effusion.

Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of 2001-08-09,
right ventricle is now smaller and more vigorous.


Brief Hospital Course:
A/P: 85 woman with PNA and UTI, leading to severe sepsis.
.
# Sepsis:  Likely secondary to urinary tract infection and
pneumonia. In the past, has grown out resistant pseudomonas from
urine.  Meropenem was started, renally dosed to cover
pseudomonas and anaerobes.  Sensitivity data demonstrated
pan-resistant pseudomonas in the urine, however, in discussion
with infectious disease, in Carter sensitivities may not
appropriately reflect in Ramirez sensitivities and meropenem is
concentrated in the urine so may indeed adequately treat her
pseudomonal infection.  Given her clinical improvement on
meropenem, she was continued during her stay and will be
discharged to rehab to complete a full 14 day course.  Her blood
pressures stabilized and did not require pressors, she was in
addition maintained on Vancomycin renally dosed, as she recently
had the history of MRSA pneumonia in 2015-03-26.  She is to finish
a 14 day course of vancomycin and meropenem to be completed on
1975-12-16.
.
# CHF: Likely diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation on
recent echo)given preserved EF. Admission BNP was 5491ad82-ba81-cae7-8556-810d7e550ccb up from
b5a66f00-6969-b7b1-5c6f-f7205881ed1c on 07-29. She initially had bilateral pleural
effusions, LE edema, and ascites/anasarca seen on CT, but
patient is currently maintaining O2 saturations in the mid 90s
on RA. Once her pressures stabilized, she was actively diuresed
with 40mg Lasix IV BID with good effect.  She did transiently
have increased oxygen requirement with oxygen saturations in the
mid 80s on room air which required 4L O2 by NC to maintain O2
sats in the mid 90s.  She was given an additional dose of IV
lasix to which she responded well and again is maintaining
oxygen saturations in the mid 90s on room air.  She will be
discharged on an increased dose of lasix from her admission dose
of 40mg po bid--> 60mg po bid.  Her daily weights and ins and
outs should be closely monitored as she may require brief tid
dosing or addition of a thiazide diuretic prior to her lasix
doses.
.
# Asthma/COPD: She was taken off steroids about 1 week ago
abruptly and was restarted with a taper this hospitalization.
She finished her steroid taper while hospitalized and was
continued on fluticasone, montelukast with good effect.
.
# Pneumonia:  She demonstrated a RLL infiltrate on imaging and
given her recurrent aspirations, she was continued on meropenem
(started originally for pseudomonas urosepsis).  She was also
continued on vancomycin given her history of MRSA pneumonia.
She will need to complete a 14 day course of both antibiotics to
be completed on 1975-12-16.
.
# Recurent aspiration:  Given her recurrent aspirations/asp.
pneumonias, she is not to take anything PO. All of her
medications are to be crushed and inserted into her PEG.
.
# ARF:  Baseline in 08 0.9.  FeNa was 0.1% on admission,
and likely reflects a forward flow issue in the setting of
sepsis and CHF.  Her creatinine was 2.3 on admission which
improved with treatment of her infections and active diuresis
and was 1.4 on discharge.
.
# Steroid-induced hyperglycemia: She is on nightly lantus which
was increased due to elevated blood sugars.  She is now on 22 U
at night plus humalog insulin sliding scale.  She did have a low
blood
sugar on admission due to tube feeds being stopped at rehab, but
was within normal limits subsequently.
.
# Anemia: Baseline Hct 30. On admission, she was transfused 1
unit PRBCs. Recent iron studies 01/2018 reveal normal iron, low
TIBC, and elevated ferritin, thus appear consistent with anemia
of chronic disease.  Her hematocrit remained stable during her
admission.
.
GI Bleed: Had one episode of BRBPR post PEG tube placement and
denies any since.  She was scheduled for colonoscopy recently,
but the test was held secondary to hypoglycemia.  Her hematocrit
remained stable and she should be followed up in
gastroenterology clinic.
.
# ? neurologic event. On admission, rehab staff reported mild
right facial droop.  Seems likely a reflection of her Bell's
Palsy given mild chronic right sided deficits prior to this
episode.  The patient herself noted no new deficits during her
hospital stay.  Head CT on admission was negative for acute
bleed.  Neuro exam is back to her baseline without new focal
deficits and her right sided facial findings are also at
baseline upon discharge.
.
# Pulmonary Mass: Appears on CXR and Right hilar mass was also
seen on chest CT. She will need future follow-up imaging to
monitor for change.
.
# H/O zoster: She reported pain on her right anterior thigh at
the site of previous zoster outbreak, but no vesicles/rash were
ever visualized. She was treated with gabapentin and a lidoderm
patch.  She will be discharged on both.  Neurontin may need to
be titrated up as renal function improves as it is currently
renally dosed.
.
# FEN: She is to be continued on tube feeds with goal of
50cc/hour.  She is currently receiving Probalance full strength
and should receive a comparable solution for adequate caloric
delivery.


Medications on Admission:
Allopurinol 100mg Qday
Caltrate/Vitamin D 1tab Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med
Cardizem 90mg Q6h
Colace 100mg Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med
Jessica 60mg Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med
Advair 250/50 diskus 1 puff Q12h
Lasix 40mg Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med
Neurontin 300/300/600mg
Regular SS
Prevacid 30mg QAM
Levofloxacin 500mg QAM - to stop on 1987-06-14
Lidoderm patch 5% Qday
Singulair 10mg Qday
MVI
Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% vial - 10cc mixed w/ Prevacid
Spiriva QAM
Trazodone 50mg QHS
Dilaudid prn
Maalox PRN
Ambien prn	


Discharge Medications:
1. Therapeutic Multivitamin     Liquid [**Date Range **]: One (1) Cap PO DAILY
(Daily).
2. Calcium Carbonate 500 mg Tablet, Chewable [**Date Range **]: One (1)
Tablet, Chewable PO Q8H (every 8 hours).
3. Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 400 unit Tablet [**Date Range **]: One (1)
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
4. Diltiazem HCl 30 mg Tablet [**Date Range **]: One (1) Tablet PO QID (4
times a day).
Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
5. Docusate Sodium 150 mg/15 mL Liquid [**Date Range **]: One (1)  PO BID (2
times a day).
6. Fexofenadine 60 mg Tablet [**Date Range **]: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times
a day).
7. Fluticasone-Salmeterol 250-50 mcg/Dose Disk with Device [**Date Range **]:
One (1) Disk with Device Inhalation Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med (2 times a day).
8. Gabapentin 300 mg Capsule Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med: One (1) Capsule PO once a day.
Disp:*30 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*
9. Tiotropium Bromide 18 mcg Capsule, w/Inhalation Device Summitridge Center- Psychiatry & Addictive Med:
One (1) Cap Inhalation DAILY (Daily).
10. Lansoprazole 30 mg Tablet,Rapid Dissolve, DR Valencia: One (1)
Tablet,Rapid Dissolve, DR Valencia DAILY (Daily).
11. Lidocaine 5 %(700 mg/patch) Adhesive Patch, Medicated Valencia:
One (1) Adhesive Patch, Medicated Topical  DAILY (Daily).
12. Montelukast 10 mg Tablet Valencia: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
13. Trazodone 50 mg Tablet Valencia: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at
bedtime) as needed.
14. Zolpidem 5 mg Tablet Valencia: Two (2) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime)
as needed for insomnia.
15. Meropenem 500 mg Recon Soln Valencia: Five Hundred (500) mg
Intravenous  Q12H (every 12 hours): Last dose to be given on
1975-12-16.
16. Vancomycin in Dextrose 1 g/200 mL Piggyback [**Date Range **]: One (1)
gram Intravenous  Q48H (every 48 hours) for 8 days: To finish on
1975-12-16.
17. Heparin Lock Flush (Porcine) 100 unit/mL Syringe [**Date Range **]: Two
(2) ML Intravenous  DAILY (Daily) as needed.
18. Insulin Lispro (Human) 100 unit/mL Solution [**Date Range **]: One (1)
Subcutaneous four times a day: Per Sliding Scale.
19. Insulin Glargine 100 unit/mL Solution [**Date Range **]: One (1)
Subcutaneous at bedtime: 22 U qhs.
Disp:*1 qs* Refills:*0*
20. Lasix 20 mg Tablet [**Date Range **]: Three (3) Tablet PO twice a day.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Select Specialty Hospital - Sioux Falls & Retirement Home - 26406 Ashley Tunnel Apt. 688
Hopkinsberg, OK 69252

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary:
Urosepsis
Pneumonia
CHF
HTN
Steroid induced hyperglycemia
.
Secondary:
Lower GIB
Bell's Palsy


Discharge Condition:
Stable


Discharge Instructions:
Weigh yourself every morning, Jessica Rodriguez MD if weight > 3 lbs.
Adhere to 2 gm sodium diet
Fluid Restriction: 1500
.
Please call your doctor or return to the hospital if you develop
fevers, chills, lightheadedness/dizziness, fainting, worsening
cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, pain/discomfort/blood
with urination, blood in your stool or dark tarry colored stool
or any other symptoms that concern you.
.
Please note we have decreased your dose of gabapentin/neurontin
because your renal function is slightly decreased.  We will also
keep you on antibiotics for a full 2 week course to finish on
1975-12-16

You have a mass in your right lung. To clarify what this is, you
need to have a CT scan with contrast once you are more stable.

Followup Instructions:
Please follow up with Dr. Valencia on 04-24 at 1:30pm at his
9115 Todd Wall Apt. 508
Toddfurt, CO 16058 office.
